serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of packaged chrysanthemum tea is approximately 200 ml (200 g), equivalent to one small bottle or a standard cup.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Chrysanthemum Tea (Less Sugar, Packaged)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for packaged less-sugar chrysanthemum tea. Input record data_source field is 'hpb' with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95; values retained and mapped to RESULT template fields. Minor micronutrient values (potassium, magnesium, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174856, Chrysanthemum tea, brewed) where HPB does not publish full micronutrient panels for beverages.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened herbal beverages — analogous estimate based on low-sugar aqueous infusion with minimal sucrose
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); supported by peer-reviewed literature (Journal of Food and Drug Analysis, 2024)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain chrysanthemum tea (unsweetened) has a GI of approximately 0 as it contains negligible carbohydrates. The packaged less-sugar variant contains approximately 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 ml (5 g per 200 ml serving), primarily from a small amount of rock sugar (sucrose, GI ~65). The blended GI for this dilute solution is estimated at approximately 15 (low), consistent with the input record estimate. Glycemic load per 200 ml serving = (15 × 5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.75. This is a very low GL. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (2 mg per 100 ml; 4 mg per 200 ml serving) and absence of saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Packaged less-sugar chrysanthemum tea is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content of 4 mg per 200 ml serving is negligible, and traditional Chinese medicine attributes mild blood-pressure-lowering properties to chrysanthemum flavonoids, though clinical evidence remains limited.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 ml; 5 g per 200 ml serving) from minimal added rock sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes can consume packaged less-sugar chrysanthemum tea in moderation; the glycemic load per 200 ml serving is approximately 0.75 (very low), but patients should account for the small amount of added sugar and prefer unsweetened variants when strict glycemic control is required.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of chrysanthemum flavonoids with potential antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Packaged less-sugar chrysanthemum tea poses no cardiovascular risk from fat or cholesterol; its flavonoid content (luteolin, apigenin) may confer modest antioxidant benefit, though clinical evidence for cardiovascular protection in humans is preliminary and this beverage should be considered a complementary choice within a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Flavonoids and phytochemicals with potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chrysanthemum tea contains flavonoids such as luteolin and apigenin that have demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in vitro; while direct clinical evidence in rheumatoid arthritis is limited, regular consumption as part of a balanced diet is unlikely to cause harm and may offer mild supportive benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chrysanthemum Tea (Less Sugar, Packaged) — known in Chinese as 菊花茶 (Júhuā chá) — is a commercially packaged herbal beverage made by steeping dried flowers of Chrysanthemum morifolium in hot water with a small amount of rock sugar added for mild sweetness. It is a staple of Chinese food culture, widely consumed across Singapore, China, Hong Kong, and the broader Chinese diaspora. The drink is pale golden-yellow in colour with a delicate, lightly floral aroma and a subtly sweet, clean taste. It is naturally caffeine-free, making it suitable for all age groups and for consumption at any time of day, including late evening. Per 100 ml, the packaged less-sugar variant provides approximately 10 kcal, 0.1 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and 2.5 g carbohydrate (of which 2.2 g is sugar from the added rock sugar). Sodium is very low at 2 mg per 100 ml. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), with a glycemic load of only 0.75 per 200 ml serving, making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. In traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), chrysanthemum tea is classified as a 'cooling' beverage believed to clear internal heat, support liver and eye health, and reduce blood pressure, though these claims are supported by limited clinical evidence. Bioactive compounds including luteolin, apigenin, chlorogenic acid, and other flavonoids contribute antioxidant and potential anti-inflammatory properties. The beverage is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal or gluten-free diets. It is commonly consumed as a refreshing accompaniment to meals, as an afternoon drink, or as a post-dinner digestive beverage.
common_user_queries: Is chrysanthemum tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in a bottle of chrysanthemum tea?
What is the GI of chrysanthemum tea?

What is 菊花茶 in English?
What are the health benefits of chrysanthemum tea?

confidence_reason: Input record carries HPB Singapore as data source with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Core macros and secondary nutrients are consistent with the HPB Foodo database profile for packaged less-sugar chrysanthemum tea. Micronutrient values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central for brewed chrysanthemum tea, which is the closest approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate documented per Veritas GI policy.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — packaged chrysanthemum tea is an aqueous herbal infusion containing no fat-bearing ingredients. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as the beverage is a filtered liquid infusion with no particulate fibre. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero as chrysanthemum tea contains no dietary vitamin D. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the packaged beverage as a whole is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this product category; the beverage is predominantly water by volume but a precise water_g figure per 100 g is not available from approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae converted from input value of 15 mcg retinol activity equivalents (RAE) — note that the input field 'vitamin_a' of 15.0 likely represents mcg RAE; this has been mapped to vitamin_a_mcg_rae = 1.25 mcg RAE per 100 ml based on USDA FoodData Central data for brewed chrysanthemum tea, as the input value of 15 mcg RAE per 100 ml appears elevated relative to approved source data for a dilute aqueous infusion; the lower USDA-aligned value is used and the discrepancy is flagged for reviewer attention. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches for chrysanthemum herbal tea infusion; SNOMED CT code is null as no specific code for this beverage product exists in SNOMED CT. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific packaged product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the processing date.
